hook: You already know how to find the matrix of a linear transformation $T: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ with respect to the standard basis — just apply $T$ to each standard basis vector and stack the results as columns. But what happens when working with a non-standard basis $\mathcal{B}$ makes the matrix far simpler, perhaps even diagonal? If you have the matrix $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$ in one basis and need $[T]_{\mathcal{C}}$ in another, or vice versa, how do you convert between them without starting from scratch?
concept: Change of basis for transformations tells us how the matrix representing $T$ changes when we switch coordinate systems. If $P$ is the change-of-basis matrix from $\mathcal{B}$ to $\mathcal{C}$, then the same transformation $T$ is represented in $\mathcal{B}$ as $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1}[T]_{\mathcal{C}}P$, so two matrices $A$ and $B$ representing the same transformation in different bases satisfy $B = P^{-1}AP$, a relationship called similarity.
anatomy: The formula $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1}[T]_{\mathcal{C}} P$ has four distinct ingredients. The matrix $[T]_{\mathcal{C}}$ is the transformation matrix you already know (often in the standard basis $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{E}$). The change-of-basis matrix $P$ has as its columns the $\mathcal{B}$-basis vectors expressed in $\mathcal{C}$-coordinates; it converts from $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates to $\mathcal{C}$-coordinates. Its inverse $P^{-1}$ converts back from $\mathcal{C}$-coordinates to $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates. The result $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$ is the new matrix that acts directly on $\mathcal{B}$-coordinate vectors.

worked_example.description_latex: We are given a linear transformation $T$ and a new basis $\mathcal{B}$. Our goal is to find $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$, the matrix that represents $T$ when inputs and outputs are expressed as coordinates relative to $\mathcal{B}$. We will use the similarity formula $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1} A P$, where $A = [T]_{\mathcal{E}}$ is the standard matrix.

We read off the standard matrix $A$ by placing the images of $\mathbf{e}_1$ and $\mathbf{e}_2$ as columns. We then build the change-of-basis matrix $P$ by placing the $\mathcal{B}$-basis vectors as columns in the order they appear in $\mathcal{B}$. Notice that $P$ converts $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates into standard coordinates.

To invert the $2 \times 2$ matrix $P$ we swap the diagonal entries, negate the off-diagonals, and divide by the determinant $\det(P) = (1)(-1) - (1)(1) = -2$. Computing $P^{-1}$ correctly is critical because it performs the conversion from standard coordinates back to $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates.

We carry out the left multiplication $P^{-1}A$ first, which is a standard matrix product. It is usually easiest to do this in two stages rather than computing $P^{-1}AP$ all at once, because it keeps the arithmetic manageable and reduces errors.

Completing the product with $P$ on the right gives us the diagonal matrix $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$. The fact that the result is diagonal confirms that $\mathcal{B}$ consists of eigenvectors of $T$ — working in the right basis has completely decoupled the transformation, which is exactly why change of basis is so powerful.

partial_example.description_latex: We want to find $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$ using $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1}AP$. Start by writing down the standard matrix $A$ and the change-of-basis matrix $P$. We have $A = \begin{pmatrix}2&0\\1&1\end{pmatrix}$ and $P = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\1&1\end{pmatrix}$.
The inverse of $P$ is given here — verify it by checking that $P^{-1}P = I$. This step converts standard-basis output coordinates back into $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates.
Now compute $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1}AP$ by first finding $P^{-1}A$ and then multiplying on the right by $P$. Show all intermediate work and write the final $2\times 2$ matrix.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $T(x_1,x_2) = (4x_1 - x_2,\; 2x_2)$ and $\mathcal{B} = \left\{\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}1\\0\end{pmatrix}\right\}$. Find $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = P^{-1}AP$ where $P$ has the basis vectors as columns.

Matrices $A$ and $B$ are called similar if $B = P^{-1}AP$ for some invertible $P$. Show that $A = \begin{pmatrix}5&1\\0&3\end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix}3&1\\0&5\end{pmatrix}$ are similar by finding an explicit invertible $P$.
Suppose $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&5\end{pmatrix}$ and $P = \begin{pmatrix}1&2\\1&3\end{pmatrix}$. Find the standard matrix $A = [T]_{\mathcal{E}} = P[T]_{\mathcal{B}}P^{-1}$.

A chemist models a two-component reaction using $T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x}$ with $A = \begin{pmatrix}3&1\\1&3\end{pmatrix}$. A colleague prefers to track the sum $s = x_1+x_2$ and difference $d = x_1-x_2$. These correspond to basis $\mathcal{B} = \{\mathbf{b}_1,\mathbf{b}_2\} = \{(1,1)^T,(1,-1)^T\}$. Find $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$ and interpret the result physically.
practice_problems.answer_latex: [T]_{\mathcal{B}} = \begin{pmatrix}4&0\\0&2\end{pmatrix}; \text{ the sum grows by factor 4 and difference by factor 2 — the dynamics fully decouple in the new coordinates.}

common_mistakes: Students often form the change-of-basis matrix $P$ using the new basis vectors as rows instead of columns; this gives the transpose of the correct $P$, producing a completely wrong similarity transform. Always place each new basis vector as a column of $P$.

Students frequently write $[T]_{\mathcal{B}} = PAP^{-1}$ instead of $P^{-1}AP$, reversing the order of $P$ and $P^{-1}$; remember that the right-hand $P$ converts from $\mathcal{B}$-coordinates to standard coordinates first, so it must appear on the right.

When asked to go from $[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$ back to the standard matrix, students mistakenly apply $P^{-1}AP$ again instead of the reverse formula $A = P[T]_{\mathcal{B}}P^{-1}$; keep track of which direction the conversion is running before setting up the product.
untested_variants: Finding $[T]_{\mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{C}}$ when both the domain basis $\mathcal{B}$ and the codomain basis $\mathcal{C}$ are non-standard and different from each other, requiring two separate change-of-basis matrices.
Using change of basis to diagonalize a matrix: given a matrix with known eigenvalues and eigenvectors, constructing $P$ from eigenvectors and verifying that $P^{-1}AP$ is diagonal.

Change of basis for transformations from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $\mathbb{R}^m$ where $n \neq m$, so the resulting matrices are rectangular and the formula takes the form $[T]_{\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} = Q^{-1}AP$.
Working in bases defined by polynomials or other abstract vector spaces (e.g., $\mathcal{P}_2$), where the coordinate vectors must first be extracted before the similarity formula can be applied.
